replies.author: @corebet5317
replies.text: The unanswered and the unknown is not just the point of cosmic horror, it is the point of horror in general. If you start explaining too much, the story is no longer horror. Something is killing people in the forest; that's horror. An alien from another planet is kiling people in the forest; that's science fiction. A serial killer is killing people in the forest; that's a crime thriller. A demon from hell is killing people in the forest; that's dark fantasy. Even the very creator of cosmic horror H.P. Lovecraft himself steered from horror into sci-fi genre in his final works just by simply explaining too much.

replies.author: @MunkeeFWRrng

replies.author: @Flare-s3c
replies.text: @corebet5317 That's exactly it, horror lives and dies by terror that the person experiencing it builds up with their own imagination, because whatever one comes up with will always be the scariest thing for them, because it's what they are most afraid of when trying to come up with an explanation for what they're seeing. As soon as it becomes a concrete thing, it *might* become scarier for some, but it will also become mundane for others.

replies.author: @thatf_inguy8220
replies.text:
I knew nothing of Markiplier and the game before seeing the movie.  I did know that it was a youtuber who self funded a movie and that was good enough for me.  I walked out pretty confused after the movie.  It was reasonably well done but it didnt really feel like a compelling story.  I was thinking "why would someone want so desperately to make this into a movie".  But a couple days later and Im liking it more and more for exactly the reason you said.  Im sick of being spoonfed the narrative in every show and movie.  Its clear that the studios have caught on to our dwindling attention spans and have changed the way they make content.  And the content is so much worse for it.  We do need more shows and movies that simply depict a story and let you take from it whatever you want.  

The problem is that this is a tough sell when movie tickets are nearly $20/pc.  I truly hope theres more movies like this and I will pay up, even if it means occasionally scratching my head in confusion when the credits roll.

replies.text:
AI is only going to open more doors in this regard.  YouTube is the perfect platform for them to share and monetise their endeavours.

I saw this tweet the other day and it sums it up perfectly:

"You watch as the old world of media crumbles before your eyes, this behemoth built on scarcity and impossible budgets, on endless armies of exhausted talent working under fluorescent lights. You remember how it used to be. Years waiting for the right people to say yes, for funding to materialize, for distribution deals to align like reluctant stars.

But now you stand at the edge of transformation, watching as small bands of warriors accomplish in weeks what once demanded years. They move like water through the cracks of the old system, unburdened by legacy, armed with nothing but talent and new tools that would have seemed like magic just years ago.

You see them clearly: the unknown creatives becoming emperors overnight. Small teams achieving what once required the resources of small nations, the best ideas rising not because they had the deepest pockets or the strongest handshakes, but because they are simply the best ideas.

The future unfolds before you like a map being redrawn in real time, and you understand that there will be nowhere to hide, no excuses to make. Only stories waiting to be told by those brave enough to embrace this new world."


- Cristóbal Valenzuela

author: @deputyrook6232
text:
a couple of details:

the planets of blood appeared shortly after the Quiet Rapture, and the reason they're sending people into those planets of blood is to gather ressources and information about where they came from and what connection they have to the Quiet Rapture. the game is purposefully obscure and mysterious to instill a deep (heh) sense of "What the actual F is going on, here?" before the ship is attacked by a monster. so i'd say a more accurate response than "Don't know" is "That's what we're trying to find out."

and the reason they're sending convicts to investigate in rusting breaking appart and barely functioning machinery instead of trained professionnals in actually well built submarines is because those expiditions serve a double function as execution methods. they don't expect nor want the protagonist to come back.

author: @DanDahMan
text:
The fact that the movie was made in the name of passion rather than profit and managed to achieve both is SO refreshing. It feels like it's been over a decade since someone made a movie simply to make a good movie. For that I can forgive all of Iron Lung's relatively inconsequential faults and boost it up to an easy 11/10. Hollywood can suck it.

Also, shout out to my coworker for calling our local theater and convincing the owner to run Iron Lung. A lot of the movie's success comes down to similar acts by dedicated Markiplier fans across the country.

replies.author: @nvelsen1975
replies.text:
Margin Call was mostly shot in one floor of one office building.

Of course they had an all-star cast on the cheap, as they stood behind the message, so people like Kevin Spacey, Jeremy Irons, Stanley Tucci on, with Paul Bettany and Zachary Quinto (who at the time, hadn't had any major roles yet but were obviously talented) worked for a lot lower a salary than they normally would.

Excellent writers too, who are able to introduce a character, and within the span of roughly 30 seconds establish his position, relevance, personality, and then have him kick the plot forward. Something which someone like Irons can do, but not with a garbage script that doesn't establish his character first. If it was a bad writer and there was just a sign "CEO" at the table it would've been a disaster. Now you see that scene literally popping up on management and leadership courses.

text: The horror genre really works in the film's favor with the light sprinkling of lore, I think. In many cases, the more you understand something, the less you fear it. It's our nature to want answers, and it's uncomfortable when we are unable to have them.

replies.author: @Geminus-123
replies.text: We got just enough information through dialogue and visual storytelling to make sense of what is happening, but not enough to lose the mystique of the world. Heck, the reason I found myself thinking about the movie days after watching it (besides being flooded by content) is exactly due to not getting all the explanation, but enough to let my mind chew on it. I also appreciate that we learn about the main characters (Simon and Ava) through natural dialogue and their reactions to events rather than "as you know" exposition.

replies.author: @Jesse-ej6jo
replies.text:
I feel incredibly old saying this, but I remember when movies used to be (~)90 minutes (according to Google, the average movie length between 2000 and 2010 was about ~90-120minutes) 
And well, "back in my day" we were happy with that. 
I honestly feel like that is a good length for a lot of films even nowadays, sure some "need"/warrant being around or slightly over 2hours, but we don't actually have to make every single film at minimum 2.5hours long. 

With that said; I personally feel like Iron Lung warrants it's runtime. It truly doesn't drag on, according to me, at all. The performance of Mark is engaging and the plot is ever moving forward. 
Sure there are "low-points", where you might say a scene or two could've been cut, but at the same time the monotonouness is deliberate to put you(the viewer) in the same headspace as the main character.

text: An understated aspect of this movie is that the crew that worked on it all seem to have absolutely LOVED working on it! Apparently the set-up of all the different stations in the movie studio was unconventional but great for crew motivation and morale, as was the incredible catering that Markiplier arranged. One of the crew was even for the first time inspired to make gifts (unique custom Iron Lung fridge magnets) for every crew member! It all just shows that while Markipliers name and face is on this project, this was truely a creative endeavour that EVERYONE involved enjoyed working on!

author: @AMainProductions
text: 1:30 the lore is described in the game, you'd have to play it to get the whole picture. But as a brief explanation the last few stations in space linked together into space cities, the problem was resources began to run scarce and the tension led to rebellions of which the protagonist was a part of. he was used as a scout because they had no qualified people left to expend on the suicide mission after years of death and survival. The expedition to the blood planets in search of a way to save humanity didn't come out of left field and it wasn't the first, the blood moons only showed up after the quiet (I prefer silent) rapture so naturally the survivors were curious and thought they might hold answers which they partially did. Several subs were sent before the events of the game and relayed images of strange alien structures and ruins which lead to even more curiosity on behalf of the survivors thus leading to the events of the game.

author: @real_abender
text:
Two important details here:

First, Iron Lung is a video game. Mark based the film off an indie horror game made by a single dev, and it is INCREDIBLY faithful to the source material. It shares the same general lore and the submarine set piece is down pat with how it looks in the game. The radiation camera comes directly from the game, and was never a cop-out for low budget. Mark then took the concept further with his own lore embellishments that the dev approved of.

Second, Markiplier is by no means an amateur when it comes to high value productions. Two of his most ambitious projects, "A Heist With Markiplier" and "In Space With Markiplier," are both CYOA miniseries that you can find on his channel, and they're incredibly well made with more elaborate sets and larger casts. Both are worth the watch to see his filmmaking origins.

SPOILERS




So simon is part of a rival station with….differing views on the rapture and how to deal with it. He was part of a crew to destroy a rival station. Really, thats about all there is to it. Though, the main character, according to the lore, wasn’t entirely on board with his home stations philosophy, which mark does an excellent job portraying.

replies.author: @Hexflame
replies.text:
Spoilers:






I mean, we find out he was part of, essentially, a cult called Eden, raised by them even. They were obsessed with trees and human life becoming the soil that would allow them to grow. Extremely nihilistic, very much humanity is over, but we can start new life by propagating trees across the cosmos and watering them with our blood style thing. Last tree of Eden died and I guess they went full nihilist, decided to kill that station, with Simon objecting. Apparently he was actually a good fighter back in the day, had a lot of blood on his hands, so 'butcher lost his nerve' and stuff like that were tossed at him by his fellows. Mixed in with all of that was a mention by one of the voices in his head that they were the one to tell him to splice the wires, which is on screen pointing to that voice being the one to tell him how to access the computer, but also seems like it could indicate he may have been the patsy to actually destroy the station, instead of just being left behind for having objected, since the person behind the voice may have told him to splice the wires back on the station as well, resulting in its death. Not sure on that second part, that one is definitely 'a theory,' but there was a lot there to play with regarding his character that I see a lot of people seemingly missing, because I certainly felt like there was plenty there. There's some that isn't clarified 100%, but I feel like all of it is there, even if you have to make some guesses to bring it all together.

As an aside, I was fond of Simon's theory, even though it seems incorrect due to the information gained later in the film, that he tossed out halfway through, or so, that the quiet rapture didn't actually steal away the planets and stars, but actually stole the barren rocks and stations. How that made just as much sense, how that would mean humanity would go on even if all of them died, and even that, if this happened once, why couldn't the reverse happen, with everything coming back. He clearly does care about more than just his own life, in spite of how important that and his freedom is to him, so it helps build the stage for his decision at the end of the film. Though, interestingly, his own cult history also helps with that, since he can use his death, 'soil,' as something to potentially help the rest of humanity, 'the tree,' grow. I thought it was fairly well interconnected to give him a reason to spit in the eye of the eldritch by the end of it all.

author: @justadude3789
text:
Mark has been working his way up to this for years. He has many projects on his channel that could be considered short films/indie productions. Like "Wilford 'Motherloving' Warfstache" which has a fun blend of comedy and drama. Or his multiple choose your own adventure series like "Who killed Markiplier" "Heist with Markiplier" and "in space with Markiplier" (the last getting a nomination for an Emmy) all creatively using youtubes format to allow the viewer to choose what happens next with multiple hidden options and paths. 

He is far more creative and passionate than Chris Stuckmann. One is desperate to cram himself into the movie industry box no matter the consequences, short cuts, or limitations. The other forced the movie industry box to open up and fit him in whether they liked it or not. One treats being a youtuber like a stepping stone to get to where he wants and would willingly drop it, and the fans that brought him up, the moment he found success in Hollywood. The other treats being a youtuber and his fans as a foundation and something to take pride in.

author: @andrewryan1946
text:
I do not watch Markiplier and have not played the game, but here is what I understood from the movie:

1:05 It is literally the Rapture. As in the biblical sense. The End Times. 

1:20 Simon was part of a group of Eden soldiers who attacked Filament Station for ideological and practical reasons (supplies to grow more trees?), but were pushed into a corner and sabotaged the reactor in order to buy time to escape but it went terribly wrong.

1:35 It is the viscera of all the humans who ‘died’ in the Quiet Rapture (again, very Biblical), and they sent Simon because previous expeditions with trained science personnel went missing (The SM-8 sub he finds) and they cannot afford any more losses of valuable and vanishingly rare experts. So they send a convict in a welded scrap sub to investigate last known coordinates for any trace of the missing science sub, or what might have destroyed it.

I won’t explain the rest for spoiler reasons, but I will say it’s fairly clear the souls of the damned inhabit the ocean of blood and coalescence into the horrors below, and God (or a god of some kind) is involved.

author: @qrzone8167
text:
I have a small interest in space stuff, and one thing I found interesting was the name of Filament station itself. Cosmic filaments were once theorized but now recently confirmed to essentially be massive hydrogen web like structures that kind of interconnect between all galaxies in the universe. Cosmic filaments are sort of a stabilizing existence for galaxies. Without them, galaxies could not continue to grow, and galaxies would become a tree without roots figuratively.  So if Filament station's name is intentional, then its destruction would essentially symbolize isolation and cosmic decay.

I would also say that because the film frequently uses light as symbolism and due to its religious themes. The destruction of a lightbulb filament (filament station) represents the light of civilization being snuffed out. In any case there are many ways to interpret it and I am overall really satisfied with the lore of Iron Lung

author: @shotaaizawa1206
text:
I’m glad this movie is getting the love it absolutely deserves.
I saw it twice and I can safely say as someone who nothing of the lore it was surprisingly incredibly competent and convincing. I figured in my first watch this was one of those movies that needed a second rewatch to fully grasp and appreciate it, and my second viewing definitely confirmed that for me. I know for many that isn’t something one wants if they didn’t like the first time around but I really do heavily recommend another rewatch as it feels so great to not only catch on details you missed or partially got much easier but elements hit more and have a surprisingly very intriguing pull to enjoy the characters even more.
Spoilers ahead with a synopsis for those who have either seen it or don’t mind, though if you are the latter I do heavily recommend a watch. The whole movies events and lore seem to relate pretty heavily to that of the Book of Revelation, THE apocalypse book, if it expanded further in the universe. Many references such as the Quiet Rapture having shared elements to the Rapture, the Suns and stars disappearing or “turn to black” while the moon “turns to blood,” blood oceans, wether God exists, a group called Eden, etc. the movie seems to use a lot of references to it as a basis of the lore applied on his humanity had expanded to the stars. As for why humanity chose this moon in particular is because it is the one that had gained a blood ocean all across the surface.
Simon is a convict that was part of Eden, which seems to be a sort of terrorist group that was a mix of both hopeful and nihilistic. Simon himself, while a good guy, wasn’t the most innocent of men, having gained the nickname “Simon the Butcher” due to his high body count. However, Simon’s conscious eventually won out and he finally confronted his brothers and group when they were going to destroy Filament Station. Unfortunately it still blew up, and he was punished for it while the others that were more guilty likely died in the explosion. I think this actually helps build Simon as a character as it shows there’s more depth to him than just being a convict: he grew up raised by a group of harmful people that finally, despite his brutal nature, stood up for what was right but too late.
In order to gain his freedom, he was given a rundown and training on how to use the Iron Lung in order to explore a particular area on the moon. Simon himself found several interesting subjects at the bottoms of the ocean but was later attacked when he tried to bring the giant skeleton up. During the whole movie, despite him being alone in the Iron Lung, he takes a surprising beaten due to rough treatment of the submarine from outer forces. He bites he likely had been suffering from a concussion, though it is likely his mind had been influenced by a greater being that swam around him.
He finds his way out the cave while also finding a specific submarine within it that, unknown to him, plays a very important part to everything. He learns of another survivor at the bottom of the sea with him that had somehow survived all that time despite having been missing for a while. She tells him if he’s desperate enough, meet her at as specific coordinate as that area is not only how she survived but was one of the partial reasons for how the Quiet Rapture had began. Simon goes towards the location, though he quickly realizes that the voice wasn’t coming from the radio, and the further he got towards the area, the more odd phenomenon occurred. A Light that Pierced the Veil was at the destined location, but what ever brought him there had only meant to play with him and try to take his mind.
Simon gets back in contact with the woman who sent him down there, Ava, despite the fact he should have died days ago from a lack of oxygen. In his anger and frustration, he exclaims the hell he’s suffered, which led back to him mentioning the SM8 (if I recall the name correctly). Ava, originally unable and unwilling to risk more men on the expedition made a desperate plea to get to the area and wirelessly download the sub’s information due to the fact it contained the answers that would greatly explain much of what occurred and potentially how for humanity to survive. Ava is a great character in my eyes as she’s really not a bad person but is also trying her best to take calculated risks and choices that would use less people for more results. Despite that, what ever was at the sub made her decide bringing Simon back was enough for her to get in a sub and meet him directly to retrieve it after the information was downloaded.
I won’t spoiled anymore, I just wanted to emphasize I really liked the world building and characters a lot. If you haven’t, check it out please.

replies.author: @felixjohnson3874
replies.text:
​@disasterincarnateit is probably one of the best Time Travel/Multiverse stories to exist, and I say this as someone with little to no care for Markiplier as a creator generally. (I mean that literally, I don't hate the guy and I've liked a lot of his stuff, but if he stopped posting tomorrow I'd just shrug, say "huh, that sucks I guess", and move on.)

It's been years since I played/watched it and I still remember that diner scene at the end, plus the fact that the 'twist' was actually fully predictable, but still had enough uncertainty that you couldn't be 100% sure that you were making the right call.

Honestly even compared to something like Everything Everywhere all at Once I think it wins by a landslide. (Tho tbf I see a lot more problems in that movie than most people seem to.)

author: @dinobro5242
text: It’s funny how your overall summary of “It’s not perfect, but it’s very enjoyable” lines up with Primitive War. Seems like we might be seeing an increase in smaller budget indie films which aren’t prefect yet deliver solid cinema experiences. If Hollywood is only going to give us generic slop, I’m more than happy to watch 7-8/10 films like these. Primitive War was the most fun I had watching a movie in a decade and Iron Lung was a genuinely suspenseful thriller. Both are better films than anything else I’ve seen in a while

author: @Krystalmyth
text:
Considering how authentic it is to the source it's probably one of the best video game adaptations ever made imo. I don't mean that hyperbolically. As a video game being made into a movie could you really ask for a more sincere adaptation? 

Imagine any other game receiving anything resembling this level of passion on the big screen. Silent Hill. Resident Evil, etc; as much fun as they might have been, they never pretended to honor those stories as we knew them. This movie loves the source, and it brought it to the masses, and improved upon its already lofty concept. That's just so rare.

author: @KerbosYT
text:
Oh man I wish the movie somewhat incentives people to play the game because as you noted there are a few details missing in the movie, for example, why they send convicts? 3 reasons, because there's not enough resources to build a proper 100% fail safe submarine to begin with, second, Simon is not the first, the others never came back (they lost a good real scientist team there) and third the human population is ridiculously low making the pool of expendable candidates equal to none.

Other example is the oceans of blood, no one knows what caused the silent rapture and no one knows why the ocean in that moon is made of blood, what makes it even more creepy is that it was corroborated that IT IS 100% human blood, on a god damn random moon, which is why they're so convinced this will give them answers that might save the human race from extinction.

All these details are found in game after you tinker around with very obscure command lines in the computer aboard, so you can even complete the game (or watch the movie) without ever knowing all this info.
